entries.title: Start with upload, then review the first useful screens
intents: what do i do first
where should i start
how do i begin
what should i do first in this product
example_questions: How do I begin with this tool?
entries.answer: Begin in Compare Prices. The first job is simply to get a clean file into the product and make sure the required columns are mapped correctly. After that, move through the comparison view before using Product History or Recipes.
entries.next_step: Open Compare Prices and complete the upload and mapping flow first.
workflow_steps: Upload a CSV or Excel pricing file in Compare Prices.
Confirm Product Name, Supplier, Unit, Quantity, Unit Price, and Date before continuing.
Use the comparison screen to review like-for-like supplier rows.
Open Product History only when you want extra trend context for a product.

entries.title: Upload a file and confirm the mapping
intents: how do i upload a file
how do i upload data
how do i import a file
how do i confirm the mapping
how do i review mappings
example_questions: How do I upload a file?
How do I confirm the column mappings?
How do I import pricing data?
entries.answer: Treat upload and mapping as a setup task. Add the file, review the detected columns, and make sure each required field points to the correct source before you trust anything else on the screen.
entries.next_step: Upload the file and review each required mapping one by one.
workflow_steps: Upload the supplier pricing file in Compare Prices.
Review the required field mapping before analysis.
Fix any wrong matches for product, supplier, unit, quantity, unit price, or date.
Start analysis only after the required fields are correct.

entries.title: Compare suppliers on the same product and unit
intents: how do i compare suppliers
how should i compare suppliers here
how do i compare supplier pricing
supplier comparison
example_questions: How should I compare suppliers here?
How do I compare supplier pricing?
entries.answer: The key idea is consistency. Supplier comparison is useful only when the same item is being viewed on the same unit and buying basis. Once that is true, you can read the comparison screen as a direct price check instead of guessing whether the rows are really equivalent.
entries.next_step: Use the comparison view and check unit consistency before comparing prices.
workflow_steps: Open a product that appears across multiple suppliers.
Confirm the same unit and quantity basis before trusting the price gap.
Rank suppliers by unit price for that product.
Use the result to decide whether to switch suppliers or renegotiate.

entries.title: Rows are not comparable when the buying basis is different
intents: why are some rows not comparable
why are rows not comparable
why cant some rows be compared
why do some rows not compare
example_questions: Why are some rows not comparable?
Why can't some rows be compared?
entries.answer: A row becomes non-comparable when the buying basis changes. That usually means the unit, quantity, or mapping is different enough that the screen cannot safely present the rows as a direct side-by-side supplier decision.
entries.next_step: Review the unit, quantity basis, and mapped fields before treating the rows as directly comparable.
workflow_steps: Review the product name and unit together.
Check whether the quantity basis is the same across suppliers.
Confirm the required fields were mapped correctly.
Only treat normalized rows as direct supplier comparisons.

entries.title: Product History shows price movement for one product over time
intents: what does product history show
what does the product history show
how does product history help
what is product history for
entries.answer: Product History is a context screen. It helps you read how one product’s visible price changes over time so you can tell whether you are looking at a repeated pattern, a temporary spike, or something that needs a second look before you act.
entries.next_step: Open Product History when you want time-based context for a product you are already reviewing.
workflow_steps: Choose a product that stands out in Step 3-Full table section.
Open Product History for that product. You can also double click a product name in the product history screen to open Product History for that product.
Check whether the higher price repeats across dates or suppliers.
Use that pattern to decide whether you are seeing drift, timing, or a one-off purchase.

entries.title: Reset all data clears active analysis, not every saved workspace record
intents: what does reset all data remove
what does reset all data do
what happens when i reset all data
what does reset remove
example_questions: What does Reset all data remove?
What happens when I use Reset all data?
entries.answer: Use Reset all data when you want a clean working state for a new upload. Think of it as clearing the active comparison session rather than erasing every saved item across the product.
entries.next_step: Choose Reset all data only when you are ready to replace the current active analysis session.
workflow_steps: Use Reset all data when the current uploaded pricing set is no longer the one you want to analyze.
Expect the active analysis context to clear.
Re-upload a new file if you want fresh comparison results.
Check separately saved workspaces like Notes or Recipes if you want to confirm what still remains.

entries.title: Saved recipes stay available after reset
intents: do saved recipes stay after reset
are saved recipes kept after reset
does reset remove saved recipes
what happens to saved recipes after reset
example_questions: Do saved recipes stay after reset?
Does Reset all data remove saved recipes?
entries.answer: Yes. Saved recipes remain in the Recipe Archive after reset. Reset affects the active analysis workspace, while the archive keeps recipe snapshots available for reopening later.
entries.next_step: Open Recipes and use the archive if you want to revisit a saved recipe after reset.
workflow_steps: Reset the active analysis only when you want a fresh pricing source.
Return to Recipes after reset.
Open the saved snapshot archive to find existing recipes.
Recalculate later if you want those recipes against a new uploaded price set.

entries.title: Uploaded price data supports comparison, history, and recipe costing
intents: what can i analyze from uploaded price data
what can i learn from uploaded price data
what does uploaded pricing data show
what can this product analyze from my uploaded file
example_questions: What can I analyze from uploaded price data?
What can I learn from my uploaded pricing file?
entries.answer: Uploaded price data powers three main kinds of work in the product: supplier comparison in Compare Prices, time-based review in Product History, and recipe costing in Recipes. In other words, one upload can support comparison, interpretation, and recipe pricing workflows.
entries.next_step: Start in Compare Prices, then branch into Product History or Recipes depending on the task you want to do.
workflow_steps: Use Step 3 to review comparable supplier price gaps.
Check non-comparable rows before trusting a savings number.
Use Product History to inspect movement over time for one product.
Open Recipes if you want to apply the uploaded pricing data to recipe costing.
